title: Understanding Errors and Exceptions
subtitle: Learn to read error messages and understand what went wrong
learning_objectives: Understand the difference between SyntaxError and runtime errors
Recognise and understand the six most common Python exceptions
Read an error message and identify the line that caused it
Fix common errors by understanding what they mean

sections.heading: Two Kinds of Errors
sections.content:
Python errors fall into two categories:

1. SyntaxError — Python refuses to even start running your code because the code itself is written incorrectly. It is like a grammar mistake in a sentence.

2. Exceptions (runtime errors) — The code starts running but hits a problem while executing. Python stops and shows you what went wrong and on which line.
sections.code_example:
# SyntaxError — Python won't run this at all
# print("hello"   # missing closing parenthesis

# Runtime error — runs, then crashes on line 2
x = 10
print(x + "world")  # TypeError: can't add int and str
sections.code_output: TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'int' and 'str'
sections.note: When you see an error, read it from the BOTTOM up: the last line tells you the error type and message, the lines above show you where in the code it happened.

sections.heading: The Six Most Common Exceptions
sections.content:
Here are the exceptions you will encounter most often as a beginner:

NameError — You used a variable that doesn't exist yet.
TypeError — You used the wrong type (e.g. adding a number and a string).
ValueError — Right type, but wrong value (e.g. int("hello")).
IndexError — You tried to access a list position that doesn't exist.
KeyError — You tried to access a dictionary key that doesn't exist.
ZeroDivisionError — You tried to divide a number by zero.
sections.code_example:
# NameError
print(age)          # age was never defined

# TypeError
print(5 + "hello")  # can't add int and str

# ValueError
int("abc")          # "abc" is not a number

# IndexError
my_list = [1, 2, 3]
print(my_list[10])  # index 10 doesn't exist

# KeyError
d = {"name": "Alice"}
print(d["age"])     # key "age" doesn't exist

# ZeroDivisionError
print(10 / 0)       # can't divide by zero
sections.code_output: Each of these lines would raise a different exception if run.

exercises.title: Trigger and Fix a TypeError
exercises.prompt: The code below has a TypeError. Fix it so it prints: My age is 25
exercises.starter_code:
age = 25
print("My age is " + age)  # This will cause a TypeError!
hints: You can't concatenate a string and an integer with +
Convert age to a string first using str()

exercises.title: Fix an IndexError
exercises.prompt: The code below crashes with an IndexError. Fix it to print the LAST item in the list (which is 'cherry').
exercises.starter_code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print(fruits[3])  # This causes an IndexError!
hints: The list has 3 items, so valid indexes are 0, 1, 2
Index 3 doesn't exist — the last valid index is 2
You can also use fruits[-1] to get the last item

quiz.question: Which type of error prevents Python from running your code at all?
quiz.explanation: SyntaxError means your code is written incorrectly (like a grammar error). Python checks the syntax before running anything, so the program never starts. All other exceptions happen while the code is running.

quiz.question: What exception does Python raise when you do: my_list = [1, 2, 3]; print(my_list[5])?
quiz.explanation: IndexError is raised when you try to access a list index that doesn't exist. The list [1, 2, 3] has valid indexes 0, 1, 2 — index 5 is out of range.

completion_message: Great — you now understand what common Python errors mean and how to read them!
